---
title: "Science as a Public Good"
subtitle: "Sessions 5 & 6 · Tue Sep 15 & Thu Sep 17"
description: "Why markets underprovide basic research, how scientific norms solve part of the problem, and the institutions that emerged from Vannevar Bush's 1945 report."
order: 5
---

## A warning from 1898

On the evening of September 7, 1898, Sir William Crookes walked onto a stage in Bristol and told an audience of thousands of scientists that Europe was going to run out of food.

The problem was nitrogen. Crops need it to grow. Until the late nineteenth century, the world's supply came mainly from guano deposits on Pacific islands and saltpeter mines in Chile, both finite, both nearly exhausted. Crookes had done the arithmetic. At current rates of population growth and nitrogen consumption, he calculated, the wheat supply would collapse sometime after 1931. "England and all civilized nations," he warned, "stand in deadly peril."

Then he said something unexpected. He did not call for population controls or rationing. He called for chemistry. Nitrogen makes up 78 percent of the atmosphere, he pointed out. If someone could find a way to fix atmospheric nitrogen into a form that plants could use, the crisis would be averted. He was describing, without knowing it, a problem that would define the next half-century of industrial chemistry.

Fourteen years later, the German chemist Fritz Haber and industrialist Carl Bosch had done it. Their process for synthesizing ammonia from nitrogen and hydrogen, the Haber-Bosch process, is now one of the most important industrial processes in the world. It consumes roughly 1 percent of global energy. Without it, roughly half the people alive today could not be fed. Haber won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1918. Bosch won it in 1931.

Here is the question this session is about: **Why didn't a private company solve this problem before Haber?**

The nitrogen crisis was well-known by the 1870s. The financial returns to solving it would have been enormous. Private firms had every incentive to invest in the research. And yet, for decades, nothing happened. It took a publicly funded chemist working at a German university, supported by government research infrastructure, to crack it.

To understand why, we need to look more carefully at what kind of thing scientific knowledge is.

::: {.instructor}
Spend 5 minutes on the Crookes story before revealing that Haber solved it. Let students sit with the question "why didn't a private company do this?" — write it on the board and come back to it after the public goods framework. This story sets up the entire session.

Connection to Crawford's "Solutionism" (Module 5 reading): Crookes is the archetype of what Crawford calls a solutionist — pessimistic about the problem, optimistic about the solution. Worth flagging briefly.
:::

## What kind of thing is knowledge?

Consider two objects. The first is a bag of nitrogen fertilizer. If you spread it on your field, your neighbor cannot spread that same fertilizer on theirs. Economists say fertilizer is **[rival]{.term data-example="A bag of fertilizer: if you use it, it is gone. One person's use reduces what is available for others."}**: one person's use reduces what is available for others.

Now consider the knowledge of *how to make* nitrogen fertilizer — the Haber-Bosch process. Once Haber published his results, every chemist in the world could study them. Carl Bosch's industrial scale-up at BASF drew on Haber's published findings. Every fertilizer plant built since has drawn on the same knowledge. Haber's use of the idea did not reduce Bosch's, or anyone else's. Knowledge is [**nonrival**]{.term data-example="The Haber-Bosch process: Haber's discovery could be studied and used by every chemist simultaneously. His use of the idea did not reduce anyone else's access to it."}.

Knowledge is also often [**nonexcludable**]{.term data-example="Once Haber published his findings, it was very hard for BASF to prevent other firms from learning and adapting the chemistry. Publication makes exclusion nearly impossible."}. Once a discovery is published (and the norms of science strongly push toward publication), it is very hard to stop others from building on it. You cannot un-read a paper.

Together, these features make scientific knowledge a [**public good**]{.term data-example="Basic scientific knowledge: nonrival (your understanding of nitrogen chemistry doesn't reduce mine) and largely nonexcludable (once published, very hard to restrict access). This is exactly why [**markets**]{.term} tend to underprovide it."}: nonrival and nonexcludable. And this creates a fundamental economic problem.

> If you cannot capture the full benefits of producing something, you will tend to produce too little of it.

This is why private firms underinvested in nitrogen chemistry for decades. The expected return to any single firm was far less than the social value of the discovery, because most of that value would immediately spill over to competitors, farmers, consumers, and governments worldwide. [**Basic research**]{.term data-example="Haber's chemistry was basic research — theoretical work on nitrogen fixation with no guaranteed commercial application. Its value only became clear years later, and far exceeded what any single funder could capture."} is especially prone to this problem: the gap between the private return and the social return is widest precisely because the knowledge is most fundamental and most freely shared.

::: {.discuss}
Haber-Bosch was eventually developed by a private company (BASF), not directly by government. Does that contradict the public goods argument? What conditions allowed a private firm to succeed where others hadn't, and what does that tell us about *when* markets can and cannot solve the problem?
:::

```{=html}
<div class="quiz">
  <p class="quiz-q">Which of the following best explains why markets underinvest in basic research?</p>
  <ol class="quiz-opts">
    <li data-correct="false" data-fb="Basic research is actually very expensive — this is not the reason markets underinvest.">Basic research is too cheap to be profitable.</li>
    <li data-correct="false" data-fb="Close, but the problem is not that the knowledge cannot be produced — it is that its benefits spill over to others who do not pay for it.">Firms cannot produce scientific knowledge.</li>
    <li data-correct="true" data-fb="Correct. Because knowledge is nonrival and nonexcludable, competitors benefit from a firm's research investment without paying for it. The private return is less than the social return, so markets underprovide.">The social return to research exceeds the private return — benefits spill over to those who did not fund the work.</li>
    <li data-correct="false" data-fb="Scientists do have incentives — the norms of priority and recognition provide them. The problem is on the funding side, not the scientist side.">Scientists have no incentive to do research.</li>
  </ol>
</div>
```

::: {.instructor}
Common confusion here: students often think "BASF did it, so markets work." The nuance is that BASF benefited from Haber's publicly-funded university research, from government-funded infrastructure, and from the wartime industrial mobilization context. The public goods problem explains underinvestment in early-stage research, not in commercial scale-up ([**applied research**]{.term data-example="BASF's scale-up of Haber's chemistry into industrial ammonia production — taking existing basic knowledge and engineering it into a manufacturable product."}). Nelson (1959) is precise on this distinction.
:::

## What [**institutions**]{.term data-example="The NSF and NIH are institutions — formal rules and organizations created specifically because markets failed to fund basic research. Bush's 1945 report was an argument for building them."} filled the gap?

If markets underprovide [**basic research**]{.term}, two kinds of institutions have historically filled the gap: **public funding** and **scientific norms**.

The public funding story in the United States traces directly to Vannevar Bush's 1945 report, *Science, The Endless Frontier*. Bush had overseen American wartime science (radar, the Manhattan Project, penicillin production) and had seen what organized scientific effort could do. Writing to President Truman in July 1945, he made a simple argument: the same logic that justified wartime investment in science applied in peacetime. The benefits of basic research, in health, security, and [**economic growth**]{.term data-example="Haber-Bosch fertilizer enabled agricultural yields that now feed roughly half the world's population — a transformation in living standards driven by a single breakthrough in basic chemistry."}, were so large and so diffuse that private actors could never capture enough of them to justify the investment. Government had to step in.

Bush's report led, eventually, to the creation of the **National Science Foundation** in 1950 and the major expansion of the **National Institutes of Health**. The pattern it established, government funding of investigator-led basic research carried out mainly at universities, shaped American science for the next seventy-five years.

But public funding alone doesn't explain how science works. Scientific research is not centrally planned. No government agency decides what questions chemists or physicists should ask. So how does a decentralized system of researchers, scattered across thousands of institutions, manage to produce cumulative, reliable knowledge?

The answer, as Nelson (1959) emphasizes, lies in the **norms of open science**: results are published, methods are disclosed, priority goes to whoever publishes first. These norms look strange from an economic perspective (why would you give away your discoveries?), but they make sense once you see them as a collective solution to the public goods problem. Because scientists compete for priority and recognition rather than for secrecy, they have strong incentives to disclose. And because they disclose, knowledge accumulates. Each generation of researchers can build on what came before without having to rediscover it.

::: {.discuss}
Bush's model — government funds basic research; private firms develop applications — has been called the "linear model" of innovation. But is it accurate? Can you think of cases where the relationship between basic research and application ran in the opposite direction, where commercial pressure drove fundamental discoveries rather than following from them?
:::

::: {.instructor}
This is a great discussion for setting up the DARPA session later. DARPA explicitly rejects the linear model — it starts from a desired capability and funds the science needed to get there. The contrast is: Bush's NSF model = investigator-driven (researchers ask their own questions); DARPA model = mission-driven (define the goal, fund the path).

Key institutional timeline for class:
- Bush report: July 1945
- NSF founded: May 10, 1950 (five years of congressional wrangling — Bush wanted civilian control, Congress wanted political oversight)
- NIH expanded: 1946–48
- DARPA (as ARPA): February 1958, response to Sputnik — a fundamentally different model
:::

## How much do governments actually spend?

The chart below shows government and private R&D expenditure as a share of GDP across major economies since the mid-1990s. Notice how the United States, Japan, South Korea, and Germany cluster around 2–3 percent, while the post-Sputnik surge in US spending — which peaked around 1964 at nearly 2 percent of GDP from federal sources alone — is not captured in data this recent.

<iframe src="https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/research-spending-gdp" loading="lazy" style="width: 100%; height: 600px; border: 0px none;" allow="web-share; clipboard-write"></iframe>

*Source: UNESCO UIS / World Bank via Our World in Data (2026). Requires an internet connection to render. Note: this chart shows total R&D (public + private). The public-private split varies significantly — the US federal share has declined from ~65% of total R&D in 1960 to around 20% today.*

::: {.instructor}
Two things to draw out from the chart: (1) the cross-country variation — South Korea and China have increased rapidly, while US federal R&D as a share of GDP has actually fallen since the 1960s; (2) the public/private mix matters as much as the total — a country could have high total R&D but most of it in commercial development rather than basic research. The Nelson public goods argument applies specifically to basic research, not to the total.
:::

## Connections

**Builds on:** The public goods framework introduced here runs directly from the discussion of externalities and market failures that students encounter in the UOE readings (Week 1–2). Knowledge spillovers are the clearest example of a positive [**externality**]{.term data-example="When Haber published his nitrogen fixation findings, the benefits flowed to every chemist, fertilizer firm, and farmer worldwide — none of whom paid for the research. That uncompensated benefit to others is the externality."} that markets undersupply.

**Sets up:** The DARPA session in Module 4 takes the Bush linear model and asks whether mission-driven science does better. The Solutionism reading (Module 5) picks up the Crookes story directly and shows what happened next.

::: {.instructor}
**Arc note:** This session is the economic foundation for two later arguments the course makes. First, that science policy choices have large, long-run consequences (Module 4). Second, that the right response to a resource constraint or [**market failure**]{.term data-example="Decades without private investment in nitrogen fixation, despite enormous potential returns — private firms couldn't capture enough of the social benefit to justify the cost. That gap is the market failure Bush's report was designed to fix."} is institutional design, not despair (Module 5 — Crawford's solutionism is exactly the attitude Vannevar Bush embodied). Students who leave without grasping the public goods argument will struggle to engage with both those later discussions.
:::

```{=html}
<div class="quiz">
  <p class="quiz-q">Vannevar Bush submitted his report <em>Science, The Endless Frontier</em> to President Truman in 1945. Which institution did his report most directly lead to creating?</p>
  <ol class="quiz-opts">
    <li data-correct="false" data-fb="DARPA was founded in 1958 — more than a decade after Bush's report, and in direct response to Sputnik rather than to Bush's recommendations.">DARPA (Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency)</li>
    <li data-correct="false" data-fb="The NIH predates Bush's report — the National Cancer Institute was founded in 1937. Bush's report gave momentum to NIH's expansion, but it did not create the institution.">The National Institutes of Health (NIH)</li>
    <li data-correct="true" data-fb="Correct. Bush's report explicitly proposed a National Research Foundation for peacetime science. After five years of congressional debate over civilian vs. political control, President Truman signed the National Science Foundation Act on May 10, 1950.">The National Science Foundation (NSF)</li>
    <li data-correct="false" data-fb="The Manhattan Project ran from 1942–1946 and preceded Bush's report. It was a wartime military program, not a product of his peacetime science policy proposal.">The Manhattan Project</li>
  </ol>
</div>

<div class="quiz">
  <p class="quiz-q">According to Nelson (1959), why do scientific norms encourage researchers to publish their findings rather than keep them secret?</p>
  <ol class="quiz-opts">
    <li data-correct="false" data-fb="This is backwards — secrecy would be the rational private strategy if profit were the only motive. Nelson's argument is that scientific norms create a different incentive.">Publishing maximises the researcher's private profit from the discovery.</li>
    <li data-correct="false" data-fb="Peer review matters for quality, but Nelson's argument about publication norms is specifically about the incentive system — not about verification.">It allows other scientists to check the work for errors.</li>
    <li data-correct="true" data-fb="Correct. Scientific norms reward priority (being first to publish) with recognition and career advancement. This gives researchers a strong incentive to disclose quickly rather than hoard. The result is that knowledge accumulates as a public good even though no individual is trying to produce a public good.">The norm of priority — recognition goes to whoever publishes first — gives researchers an incentive to disclose rather than hoard.</li>
    <li data-correct="false" data-fb="While legal requirements exist in some contexts, Nelson's argument is about the internal norms of the scientific community, not external legal mandates.">Publication is legally required for research receiving public funding.</li>
  </ol>
</div>
```

## Review cards

Work through these cards now, then Orbit will schedule them for review over the coming weeks. Studies show that spaced retrieval practice produces dramatically better long-term retention than re-reading.

```{=html}
<orbit-reviewarea color="orange">

  <orbit-prompt
    question="What does it mean for a good to be nonrival? Give an example."
    answer="One person's use does not reduce availability to others. The Haber-Bosch process is nonrival: every chemist in the world could study Haber's findings simultaneously without depleting them — unlike a bag of fertilizer, which is rival.">
  </orbit-prompt>

  <orbit-prompt
    question="What does it mean for a good to be nonexcludable? Why does this matter for science?"
    answer="A good is nonexcludable when people cannot easily be prevented from using it. Once a scientific paper is published, it is nearly impossible to stop others from reading and building on it — 'you cannot un-read a paper.' This means firms cannot capture the returns on their research investment.">
  </orbit-prompt>

  <orbit-prompt
    question="Why is basic scientific knowledge a public good?"
    answer="It is both nonrival (unlimited simultaneous use — your understanding of nitrogen chemistry doesn't reduce mine) and nonexcludable (once published, very hard to restrict access). Public goods tend to be underprovided by markets because producers cannot capture their full social value.">
  </orbit-prompt>

  <orbit-prompt
    question="Why did private firms underinvest in nitrogen fixation research before Haber, even though the financial returns would have been enormous?"
    answer="Because the knowledge of how to fix nitrogen, once discovered, would immediately spill over to competitors, farmers, consumers, and governments worldwide. The private return to any single firm was far less than the social return — the classic market failure from positive externalities.">
  </orbit-prompt>

  <orbit-prompt
    question="What was Vannevar Bush's core argument for government funding of basic research?"
    answer="The benefits of basic research — in health, security, and economic growth — are so broad and diffuse that no private actor can capture enough of them to justify the investment. Government must fund what markets will systematically underprovide.">
  </orbit-prompt>

  <orbit-prompt
    question="How long after Vannevar Bush submitted his 1945 report was the National Science Foundation created, and what caused the delay?"
    answer="Five years. Bush submitted the report in July 1945; the NSF Act was signed on May 10, 1950. The delay was caused by congressional disagreement: Bush wanted a civilian-led foundation with scientists choosing research priorities; Congress wanted more political oversight.">
  </orbit-prompt>

  <orbit-prompt
    question="Why do scientific norms push researchers to publish discoveries rather than keep them secret?"
    answer="Scientific norms reward priority — recognition and career advancement go to whoever publishes first. This gives researchers strong incentives to disclose quickly rather than hoard. The result is that knowledge accumulates as a public good even though no individual is trying to produce a public good.">
  </orbit-prompt>

  <orbit-prompt
    question="According to Nelson (1959), what is the key difference between basic research and applied research in terms of spillovers?"
    answer="The gap between private and social return is widest for basic research, because basic knowledge is most fundamental and most freely shared — competitors can build on it in ways that cannot easily be prevented. Applied research, closer to commercial products, is somewhat more appropriable.">
  </orbit-prompt>

</orbit-reviewarea>
```

## Reading guide

### Required

**Nelson, Richard R.** "The Simple Economics of Basic Scientific Research." *Journal of Political Economy* 67, no. 3 (1959): 297–306.

> **What to look for:** Nelson makes a tight economic argument in ten pages. Track the logical structure: (1) basic research produces knowledge; (2) knowledge has public good properties; (3) therefore markets underprovide it; (4) therefore universities and government fill the gap. Does each step hold?
>
> **Key argument:** The social returns to basic research systematically exceed private returns because knowledge is nonexcludable — competitors benefit whether or not they paid for the research.
>
> **Bring to class:** Nelson was writing in 1959, a year after Sputnik and during the height of Cold War science funding. Does his argument depend on that context — or is it timeless?

**Bush, Vannevar.** *Science, The Endless Frontier* (1945). Transmittal letter + Chapter 3, "The Importance of Basic Research" (~8 pp.). [Free at nsf.gov](https://www.nsf.gov/od/lpa/nsf50/vbush1945.htm)

> **What to look for:** Bush is making a policy argument to a president, not a theoretical argument to economists. Notice how he grounds the case for public funding in concrete wartime examples before pivoting to peacetime. What does he assume about how science works?
>
> **Key argument:** The benefits of basic research are so broad and so unpredictable that no private actor can capture enough of them to justify the investment — government must act as the funder of last resort.
>
> **Bring to class:** Bush says the government should fund science but scientists should choose the questions. Is that the right [**division of labor**]{.term data-example="In Bush's model: government funds basic research (scientists choose the questions), private firms develop applications. NSF funds the curiosity; BASF builds the factory."}? Who should decide what gets studied?

### Recommended

**Dasgupta, Partha, and Paul A. David.** "Toward a New Economics of Science." *Research Policy* 23, no. 5 (1994): 487–521.

> Pages 487–497 give the fullest economic account of why open science norms (publish, don't hoard) are themselves a collective solution to the public goods problem. Goes deeper than Nelson on the incentive structure of the scientific community.

**Romer, Paul M.** "The Origins of Endogenous Growth." *Journal of Economic Perspectives* 8, no. 1 (1994): 3–22.

> Connects the public goods argument in Nelson to the broader growth theory literature. Explains why non-rival ideas are at the heart of long-run economic growth. No technical prerequisites — written for a general economics audience.

**Polanyi, Michael.** "The Republic of Science: Its Political and Economic Theory." *Minerva* 1, no. 1 (1962): 54–73. [PDF via library]

> The philosophical complement to Nelson. Polanyi argues science is a spontaneous order — analogous to a market — governed by mutual adjustment rather than central direction. Read alongside Nelson to see how the economic and sociological accounts of science connect.

**Mastroianni, Adam.** "The Rise and Fall of Peer Review." *Experimental History* (2022). [experimentalhistory.substack.com/p/the-rise-and-fall-of-peer-review](https://experimentalhistory.substack.com/p/the-rise-and-fall-of-peer-review)

> A brisk, provocative read asking whether peer review — the mechanism Polanyi calls scientific self-governance — actually works. Good for students who engage quickly with the required readings and want a contemporary provocation. [Verify URL before semester.]

::: {.instructor}
**Teaching note on readings:** Nelson and Bush are doing different things — Nelson builds the theoretical case, Bush makes the policy argument. Use the first session (Tue Sep 15) for Nelson and the public goods framework, and the second session (Thu Sep 17) for Bush and the institutional history. The contrast between them is productive: Nelson says *why* markets fail; Bush says *what we should do about it*. The Dasgupta & David recommended reading bridges them.
:::
